Patch Set 7:

(1 comment)

Patch Set 11:

> actually.. I'm wondering couldn't we use OUT to notify as well. essentially this code just list (or store) some additional job. we already have something very similar - trace file, which is an index of FIDs, right? that index can be accessed from any node, in theory.

In theory, using OUT RPC for LFSCK notification is possible, but we need a new sub-code for such OUT RPC, something like OUT_NOTIFY. But because such operation is quite different from others, I am not sure whether it is the right way to do that. On the other hand, if this notification can be done via OUT RPC, then most of other LFSCK RPCs can be also, then the OUT RPC will become some kind of network ioctl. It seems not our original expectation.

Patch Set 11:

not quite. like we discussed in another patch, I think that notification can be send once. you can consider this as an additional phase. notification itself should be done with LFSCK control protocol, which is not OSD API. but what to scan can be an index which we can exchange/maintain using  OSD API. this is again the case where index updates can be batched. then with a single notification (which asks remote LFSCK instance to process additional items) it will be less RPCs, not RPC/item.

Patch Set 7:

(1 comment)

Patch Set 11:

> not quite. like we discussed in another patch, I think that notification can be send once. you can consider this as an additional phase. notification itself should be done with LFSCK control protocol, which is not OSD API. but what to scan can be an index which we can exchange/maintain using OSD API. this is again the case where index updates can be batched. then with a single notification (which asks remote LFSCK instance to process additional items) it will be less RPCs, not RPC/item.

Yes, as optimisation, we can reduce the LFSCK RPCs count via batching more LFSCK events in single LFSCK RPC. For that, we need to record the remote FIDs on this MDT temporary, and batch them together when the first-stage scanning finished. Those FIDs can be recorded in RAM only, that is simple, but if the local LFSCK crashed/stopped before the aggregated notification sent out, then related verification will be done when the LFSCK run on related MDT(s) next time. We also can record those remote FIDs on-disk, although it may introduce more complexity and overhead, it can guarantee that related verification will be done within this LFSCK run.

Patch Set 11:

frankly, I don't think we absolutely need to guarantee all the rescans within the same LFSCK run. and we don't need to save something - just do normal OSD API insert into a remote index storing FIDs to rescan. then OUT will be able to batch.

Patch Set 11:

> Patch Set 11:
> frankly, I don't think we absolutely need to guarantee all the rescans within the same LFSCK run. and we don't need to save something - just do normal OSD API insert into a remote index storing FIDs to rescan. then OUT will be able to batch.

That is also possible solution.

parents.subject: LU-5519 lfsck: repair bad name hash for striped directory

commit.subject: LU-5519 lfsck: repair master LMV for striped directory
commit.message:
LU-5519 lfsck: repair master LMV for striped directory

If the master MDT-object of a striped directory lost its LMV EA,
then there may be some users have created some files under the
master MDT-object directly. Under such case, the LFSCK cannot
re-generate LMV EA for the master MDT-object, because we should
keep the existing files to be visible to client. Then the LFSCK
will mark the striped directory as read-only and keep it there
to be handled by administrator manually.

If nobody has created files under the master MDT-object of the
striped directory, then we will set the master LMV EA and
generate a new rescan (the striped directory) request that will
be handled later by the LFSCK instance on the MDT later.

Signed-off-by: Fan Yong <fan.yong@intel.com>
Change-Id: I4d604cbd346c2cb044503f193ab4745e7fd2c2a2
Reviewed-on: http://review.whamcloud.com/11847
Tested-by: Jenkins
Tested-by: Maloo <hpdd-maloo@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Alex Zhuravlev <alexey.zhuravlev@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Lai Siyao <lai.siyao@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <oleg.drokin@intel.com>
